sources.notes: Read the full statement and bounds: k <= 100000, n <= 900000000, sum k <= 100000, and answers are required for every product x in [1,k] modulo 998244353.
The official editorial gives both intended approaches: ordered-factorization DP plus the hockey-stick identity, and a degree-at-most-17 polynomial evaluated by Lagrange interpolation.
Cross-checked the DP over products and non-one count, the insertion-of-ones interpretation, direct small-r binomial evaluation, and O(k log^2 k) implementation.
Independently cross-checked the cap of 16 non-one entries and the simplification sum_{m=1..n} C(m,j)=C(n+1,j+1).
Re-derived both formulas. The binomial-basis answer matched direct length DP for k <= 12 and n <= 7 (546 product entries), and 18-point interpolation matched direct values at n=25 for all x <= 30.

annotation.summary: For every x from 1 to k, count arrays of any length from 1 through n whose positive entries have product x, modulo 998244353.
annotation.constraints: t <= 1000; k <= 100000; n <= 900000000; sum k <= 100000. The huge n rules out DP by total length, while x <= k sharply bounds how many entries can exceed 1.
annotation.core_idea: For x>1, an array with product x has at most floor(log2 x) <= 16 entries greater than 1. Let dp[x][j] be the number of ordered j-tuples of integers greater than 1 with product x. It obeys dp[x][j]=sum_{d|x,d>1} dp[x/d][j-1], implementable by sieve-style transitions over multiples. For a total length m, choose the j non-one positions in C(m,j) ways; summing m from 1 to n and applying the hockey-stick identity gives ans[x]=sum_j dp[x][j] C(n+1,j+1). Compute these small-lower-index binomials directly modulo 998244353. The exceptional product x=1 has exactly the n all-one arrays. An official alternative observes that the exact-length count f_x(m) has degree Omega(x), so its prefix sum S_x(n) has degree at most 17 and can be evaluated from 18 small samples by Lagrange interpolation.
annotation.complexity: O(k log^2 k) time and O(k log k) memory per test in the primary approach; the sum-k bound applies across tests.
tricks: Bound the number of entries greater than 1 by 16 using 2^17 > 100000.
Count the ordered non-one subsequence before inserting ones.
Collapse the sum over all allowed lengths with the hockey-stick identity.
Evaluate C(n+1,r) from a short falling product because r <= 17 even though n is large.
Alternative: prove low polynomial degree from the proper-divisor recurrence and interpolate.
review_risks: The proposed math/polynomial-interpolation tag is new. The main agent must confirm its taxonomy path and add the WebUI Chinese translation if accepted.

solution_variants.name: ordered-factorization-dp-hockey-stick
solution_variants.summary: Precompute dp[x][j] for ordered factorizations into j factors greater than 1 using divisor/multiple transitions. Weight each j by C(n+1,j+1), obtained after summing choices of non-one positions over every allowed length; output n separately for x=1.
solution_variants.complexity: O(k log^2 k) time and O(k log k) memory.

solution_variants.name: small-degree-polynomial-lagrange
solution_variants.summary: Let f_x(m) count length-m arrays and S_x(n)=sum_{m=1..n} f_x(m). From f_x(m)-f_x(m-1)=sum_{d|x,d<x} f_d(m-1), inductively deg f_x=Omega(x) and deg S_x=Omega(x)+1 <= 17. Precompute S_x at 18 small arguments and evaluate S_x(n) with Lagrange interpolation modulo 998244353.
solution_variants.complexity: O(k log^2 k) time and O(k log k) memory with the degree bounded by 17.

tags.evidence: The main precomputation counts ordered non-one factorizations by the two-dimensional state (product x, number j of factors).
Each DP layer aggregates over proper divisors, equivalently propagating dp[p][j] to all multiples p*d, which is sieve-style computation on the divisibility poset.
After choosing positions for j non-one values, the decisive simplification is sum_{m=1..n} C(m,j)=C(n+1,j+1), eliminating the enormous length dimension.
The proof that at most 16 entries can exceed 1 is what reduces an n-sized dimension to a constant-sized factor-count dimension.
For each of O(log k) factor-count layers, transitions over products p and multipliers d cost sum_p floor(k/p)=O(k log k), yielding O(k log^2 k).
The official second solution proves S_x(n) has degree at most 17, samples it at 18 small arguments, and evaluates the requested large n by Lagrange interpolation.
tags.description: Evaluate or reconstruct a bounded-degree polynomial from sufficiently many sample values; use when proving a low degree makes a very large argument tractable.
tags.created_reason: The existing taxonomy has no interpolation tag, while the official alternative is fundamentally polynomial evaluation rather than the primary binomial-identity formula.
tags.main_agent_review_note: Confirm the taxonomy path and add the WebUI Chinese translation before accepting this candidate.
